text: Agro-processing is a high-demand micro-enterprise in Uganda, turning raw farm produce into shelf-stable products with higher margins. The three most accessible entry points for youth are cassava flour milling (producing cassava flour or HQCF — High Quality Cassava Flour), maize milling (posho flour), and groundnut paste production. Uganda produces over 5 million tonnes of cassava and 3 million tonnes of maize annually, yet most is sold raw at low prices. Processing adds 40-100% value. Startup capital ranges from UGX 3,000,000 for a manual groundnut paste operation to UGX 15,000,000-25,000,000 for a small motorised milling setup. Location near farming communities (Lira, Soroti, Masindi, Iganga) reduces raw material transport costs significantly.

Startup costs for a small cassava flour milling business: Cassava chipper/slicer (UGX 800,000-1,500,000), drying racks or tarpaulins (UGX 200,000-500,000), hammer mill or posho mill (UGX 5,000,000-12,000,000 for motorised, UGX 1,500,000-3,000,000 for manual), packaging materials and sealer (UGX 300,000-600,000), UNBS certification fees (UGX 500,000-1,000,000), initial raw cassava stock — 1 tonne at UGX 400,000-600,000. Total realistic startup: UGX 8,000,000-16,000,000 for motorised, UGX 3,000,000-6,000,000 for manual/semi-manual. For maize milling (posho), a small diesel-powered posho mill costs UGX 4,000,000-8,000,000. Groundnut paste requires a motorised grinding machine (UGX 2,000,000-5,000,000), roasting pan, and packaging — total startup UGX 3,000,000-7,000,000.

Revenue model for agro-processing: Cassava flour sells at UGX 2,500-4,000 per kg retail, versus raw cassava at UGX 400-700 per kg — a 4-5x value increase. Processing 500 kg of raw cassava yields approximately 150-180 kg of flour (30-35% conversion rate). Monthly revenue processing 2 tonnes of raw cassava: UGX 1,500,000-2,500,000 from flour sales, with raw material cost of UGX 800,000-1,200,000. Maize posho milling earns UGX 200-500 per kg as a milling service, or UGX 2,000-3,500 per kg selling packaged posho flour. Groundnut paste retails at UGX 8,000-15,000 per kg; processing 100 kg of raw groundnuts (bought at UGX 4,000-6,000/kg) into paste generates profit of UGX 300,000-500,000 per batch. Break-even for a motorised mill: typically 6-12 months with consistent supply.

Key risks in agro-processing: 1) Seasonal raw material scarcity — cassava and groundnut prices spike during planting seasons (March-April, September-October), sometimes doubling. Mitigation: buy and stockpile during harvest glut periods. 2) Power/fuel costs — diesel for mills is UGX 5,000-6,000/litre; electricity outages disrupt production. Consider solar-assisted drying and backup generators. 3) Quality rejection — if moisture content exceeds 12% in cassava flour, it spoils quickly and large buyers reject it. Invest in a simple moisture meter (UGX 150,000-300,000). 4) UNBS compliance — selling packaged food products requires Uganda National Bureau of Standards certification. Operating without it risks fines and market exclusion. 5) Competition from large millers — differentiate through freshness, local sourcing, and smaller pack sizes (500g, 1kg) for low-income buyers.

Marketing agro-processed products in Uganda: 1) Brand your product with a simple, memorable name and professional label — even small-scale producers who brand their cassava flour or groundnut paste command 20-30% higher prices. 2) Supply local retail shops, schools, and restaurants directly — they prefer reliable local suppliers over distant wholesale. 3) Sell at weekly markets in trading centres (e.g., Busia, Jinja, Mbale market days). 4) Use WhatsApp groups and Facebook Marketplace to reach Kampala buyers — urban consumers increasingly buy packaged traditional foods online. 5) Offer free samples at churches, markets, or community events to build word-of-mouth. 6) Partner with women's groups and SACCOs who buy in bulk for their members.

Equipment and infrastructure for agro-processing: For cassava flour — you need a peeling station (concrete slab with drainage), chipping machine (manual or motorised), drying area (raised racks with polythene cover for rain protection, or a solar dryer at UGX 1,000,000-3,000,000), milling machine, and storage room (dry, ventilated, off the ground). For groundnut paste — a roasting drum or large pan over a charcoal stove, a motorised grinding machine with stainless steel plates, glass jars or plastic containers with labels, and a heat sealer for sachets. For maize milling — a posho mill (hammer mill type), grain cleaner/sieve, weighing scale, and packaging supplies. All equipment should be food-grade stainless steel where it contacts the product.

Growth path for agro-processing: Start with one product line (e.g., cassava flour only) and master quality and supply consistency before expanding. Phase 1 (Months 1-6): Manual or semi-manual processing, sell locally, get UNBS certification. Phase 2 (Months 7-18): Upgrade to motorised mill, add packaging and branding, expand to nearby trading centres and Kampala distributors. Phase 3 (Year 2-3): Add a second product line (e.g., add groundnut paste to cassava flour), contract farmers for steady supply, explore export to South Sudan or DRC border markets. Phase 4 (Year 3+): Apply for Emyooga SACCO seed capital (UGX 30,000,000 for groups), form a processing cooperative to share equipment costs, target supermarket shelves (Shoprite, Capital Shoppers) and institutional buyers (schools, hospitals). The Uganda Development Corporation (UDC) and Uganda Industrial Research Institute (UIRI) offer technical support for value-addition enterprises.
